nativeRange.habitat: Wetlands, marshes, and roadside ditches across the southeastern United States; amphibious and capable of growing partially or fully submerged

decorPreferences: open background placement with room to grow tall
bright lighting to develop red/burgundy coloration

summary: A South-American-marketed (but botanically southeastern-US) red background stem plant in the Onagraceae, frequently synonymized with Ludwigia glandulosa; develops intense burgundy-to-deep-red foliage under high light, CO2, and iron-rich fertilization, with larger spade-shaped leaves than L. repens.
careNotes: Ludwigia peruensis is widely sold as a 'red Ludwigia' background stem and is treated by most aquarium sources as a trade name (or geographic form) of Ludwigia glandulosa rather than a strictly separate species — botanical references place Ludwigia glandulosa in the family Onagraceae (order Myrtales), native to wetlands and roadside ditches of the southeastern United States, despite the misleading 'peruensis' epithet. It is larger-leaved than L. repens, with small-to-medium spade-shaped leaves on upright stems that reach roughly 30-55 cm under cultivation (Buce Plant cites height as relatively compact when trimmed; Aqua-Fish.net gives 40-55 cm; Wikipedia gives ~40 cm for L. glandulosa). Sources agree on a temperature range around 22-28 °C and pH 6.0-7.5, with hardness tolerated from soft to moderately hard (~4-18 dGH). Coloration is the entire point of this plant: under low/medium light it stays olive-green to brown, but under high light (~30-50 PAR) with pressurized CO2 (~20-30 mg/L) and consistent iron + micronutrient dosing the leaves shift to burgundy, copper-red, and deep red, especially on the upper third of the stem. Nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil or root tabs) is strongly recommended; iron deficiency or chronically low light is the most common cause of failure to develop the marketed red color. Propagation is straightforward via stem cuttings — the plant is forgiving of trimming and rewards regular topping with bushier lateral growth. Like other Ludwigias, freshly planted tissue-culture or emersed-grown stems may shed their first set of leaves during the submersed transition; new submersed growth should follow within 1-2 weeks under good conditions.
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings: cut healthy stem tops or side shoots (about 5-10 cm with a few leaves), remove the lowest leaf pair, and replant into the substrate — cuttings root quickly. Regular topping of the tallest stem encourages bushier lateral side-shoot growth on the mother plant. Emersed flowering and seed propagation are not commonly used in the hobby.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Used for trade name (Ludwigia glandulosa peruensis), origin (North America), lighting (medium-high, stronger light intensifies red coloration), CO2 (recommended but not strictly required), growth rate (moderate-to-fast), placement (mid-to-background), temperature (72-82 °F / 22-28 °C), propagation (stem-top and side-shoot cuttings), fertilization (iron-rich), care difficulty (moderate), leaf shape (small spade-shaped), and color range (olive-brown to deep red/burgundy depending on light).

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Onagraceae), native range (southeastern USA), lighting (bright; green-to-red color tradeoff), CO2 (beneficial), growth rate (normal), placement (midground/background), substrate (fine gravel + supplementation), temperature (22-28 °C), pH (6.5-7.4), hardness (4-18 dGH), height (40-55 cm), and care level (medium).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Ludwigia glandulosa
additional.notes: Used as botanical reference for the senior synonym Ludwigia glandulosa: family Onagraceae, order Myrtales, native to wetlands and roadside ditches of the southeastern United States, amphibious habit (grows partially or fully submerged), maximum height ~40 cm, pH ~6-7.5, red/purple coloration with adequate light fading to green under low light. Confirms the taxonomic identity behind the 'peruensis' trade name (the South-American epithet is widely treated as a hobby misnomer).
